classSummaries.text: The first hunters learned quickly that silvered words and clever plans mean little when a Dreadblood breaks the line. Brutes are the iron answer to that horror: scarred front-line killers who meet monstrosity with muscle, rage, and the stubborn refusal to die.

Before rifles became the hunter's sacred thunder, Scouts were the first to prove that a Dreadblood could be studied, stalked, and dropped from afar. They are pathfinders, marksmen, and survivalists who read the land like scripture and kill before the dark can close its jaws.

Wardens are the steady hand in a world gone mad. Trusted more than most hunters, they command bands through discipline, tactics, and cold battlefield judgment, turning frightened killers into something sharper than fear.

When brute force and rifle fire were not enough, the bands turned to assassins, cutthroats, and thieves. Stalkers are the knives in the blind corner: silent murderers who learned that some horrors must be killed before they know the hunt has begun.

Some hunters curse the Bloodbound as fanatics, and perhaps they are right. These hunters drink from the edge of damnation, forcing Dreadblood transformations through their own flesh for a few terrible moments of power, always one heartbeat from becoming the very thing they hunt.

Every Deepcaller carries a Book of the Deepcaller, though no one knows who wrote the first, or why copies find their way to those willing to listen. Feared as lunatics and tolerated only for the impossible power they bring, Deepcallers open their minds to the things beneath thought—and sometimes, those things answer.

handbook.notes: Source: the full pdftotext re-extraction of the 89 MB handbook PDF (2873 lines), which is more complete than the pre-existing resources/extracted/text/...txt (903 lines, truncated mid-Marksman). OCR ligature artifacts were repaired throughout (e.g. proficiency, Rifle, suffer, specified, finish, effect, modifier, Significant, Insignificant, benefit, firearm, first, five, fight); 'CHR' left as in source; 'Eldrich'/'eldrich' left verbatim where the source spells it that way (it also uses 'Eldritch' elsewhere). The book has 5 chapters: 1 Creating a Character, 2 Classes (only framed here — per-class traits/features/subclasses are in separate class fragments), 3 Backgrounds, 4 Feats, 5 Equipment. NO standalone 'Sanity & Madness' chapter or 'Conditions' chapter exists in the text; those concepts appear inline (rest/Sanity Die/Hit Dice rules in Ch.1 Step 5; Madness/Transformation in Bloodvial and class features; conditions are referenced as 5e-style named conditions e.g. Prone, Restrained, Frightened, Grappled, Incapacitated). The 'Transformation Table' is referenced (Step 5) but the actual D20 results table is NOT present in the extracted text (likely an image-only page). Background Overview Part 2 was badly jumbled by OCR (multi-line cells interleaved); the reconstruction is best-effort and several Feat/Tool cells could not be reliably read — marked '(not legible in source)' or '—' and should be re-verified against the PDF image. Class Overview Part 1 starting-equipment values were taken from the better-laid-out original .txt (the re-extraction showed only 'See chapter 2'); the per-class Core Traits Sanity values were re-read directly from the handbook PDF and are accurate per class (Brute 12/2d6, Scout 12/2d6, Stalker 12/1d12, Deepcaller 16/1d20, Bloodbound 20/1d20, Warden 14/4d4); these differ from the standalone class boards captured in classes[] (single dice D10/D12/D20) for several classes — see the top-level sourceConflicts entry; confirm with the DM. Level 1/Fixed HP tables list 'Rouge' which corresponds to the Stalker (Rogue-based) class. Storage item 'BACKPAC' corrected to 'Backpack'.

classes.name: Hunter Brute
classes.flavorText: Becoming a Hunter Brute. As a level 1 character: Gain all the traits in the Core Hunter Brute Traits table. Gain the Hunter Brute's level 1 features, which are listed in the Hunter Brute Features table. As a Fighter, you gain the following class features when you reach the specified Fighter levels. These features are listed on the Fighter Features table.

overview.skillProficiencies: Choose 2: Acrobatics, Athletics, Grit, Perception, Survival or Intimidation

coreTraits.name: Skill Proficiencies
coreTraits.text: Choose 2: Acrobatics, Athletics, Grit, Perception, Survival or Intimidation

features.name: Fighting Style
features.text: You have honed your martial prowess and gain a Fighting Style feat of your choice (see chapter 4). Whenever you gain a Fighter level, you can replace the feat you chose with a different Fighting Style feat.

features.name: Weapon Mastery
features.text: Your training with weapons allows you to use the mastery properties of three kinds of Simple or Martial weapons of your choice. Whenever you finish a Long Rest, you can practice weapon drills and change one of those weapon choices. When you reach certain Fighter levels, you gain the ability to use the mastery properties of more kinds of weapons, as shown in the Weapon Mastery column of the Hunter Brute Features table.

features.name: Action Surge
features.text: You can push yourself beyond your normal limits for a moment. On your turn, you can take one additional action. Once you use this feature, you can't do so again until you finish a Short or Long Rest. Starting at level 17, you can use it twice before a rest but only once on a turn.

features.name: Refuse the Bleeding
features.text: When you take damage, you may use your reaction and expend one use of Refuse the Bleeding to reduce the incoming damage by 1d10 + your Hunter Brute level. You regain one expended use when you finish a short rest, and you regain all expended uses when you finish a long rest.

features.name: Hunter Brute Subclass
features.text: You gain a Hunter Brute subclass of your choice. The Battle Master or the Champion subclasses are detailed after this class's description. A subclass is a specialization that grants you features at certain Hunter Brute levels.

features.name: Ability Score Improvement
features.text: You gain the Ability Score Improvement feat (see chapter 5) or another feat of your choice for which you qualify. You gain this feature again at Hunter Brute levels 6, 8, 12, 14, and 16.

features.name: Extra Attack
features.text: You can attack twice instead of once whenever you take the Attack action on your turn.

features.name: War Master
features.text: When you attack with a weapon whose mastery property you can use, you can replace that property with the Push, Sap, or Slow property for that attack.

features.name: Two Extra Attacks
features.text: You can attack three times instead of once whenever you take the Attack action on your turn.

features.name: Studied Attacks
features.text: You study your opponents and learn from each attack you make. If you make an attack roll against a creature and miss, you have Advantage on your next attack roll against that creature before the end of your next turn.

features.name: Epic Boon
features.text: You gain an Epic Boon feat (see chapter 4) or another feat of your choice for which you qualify.

features.name: Three Extra Attacks
features.text: You can attack four times instead of once whenever you take the Attack action on your turn.

subclasses.name: Battle Master
subclasses.description: Master Sophisticated Battle Maneuvers. Battle Masters are students of the art of battle. The most accomplished Battle Masters are well-rounded figures who use their carefully honed combat skills.

features.name: Combat Superiority
features.text:
Your experience on the battlefield has refined your fighting techniques. You learn maneuvers that are fueled by special dice called Superiority Dice.

Maneuvers. You learn three maneuvers of your choice from the "Maneuver Options" section later in this subclass's description. Many maneuvers enhance an attack in some way. You can use only one maneuver per attack. You learn two additional maneuvers of your choice when you reach Hunter Brute levels 7, 10, and 15. Each time you learn new maneuvers, you can also replace one maneuver you know with a different one.

Superiority Dice. You have four Superiority Dice, which are d8s. A Superiority Die is expended when you use it. You regain all expended Superiority Dice when you finish a Short or Long Rest. You gain an additional Superiority Die when you reach Hunter Brute levels 7 (five dice total) and 15 (six dice total).

Saving Throws. If a maneuver requires a saving throw, the DC equals 8 plus your Strength or Dexterity modifier (your choice) and Proficiency Bonus.

features.name: Maneuver Options
features.text:
The maneuvers are presented here in alphabetical order.

Bait and Switch. When you're within 5 feet of a creature on your turn, you can expend one Superiority Die and switch places with that creature, provided you spend at least 5 feet of movement and the creature is willing and doesn't have the Incapacitated condition. This movement doesn't provoke Opportunity Attacks. Roll the Superiority Die. Until the start of your next turn, you or the other creature (your choice) gains a bonus to AC equal to the number rolled.

Disarming Attack. When you hit a creature with an attack roll, you can expend one Superiority Die to attempt to disarm the target. Add the Superiority Die roll to the attack's damage roll. The target must succeed on a Strength saving throw or drop one object of your choice that it's holding, with the object landing in its space.

Distracting Strike. When you hit a creature with an attack roll, you can expend one Superiority Die to distract the target. Add the Superiority Die roll to the attack's damage roll. The next attack roll against the target by an attacker other than you has Advantage if the attack is made before the start of your next turn.

Evasive Footwork. As a Bonus Action, you can expend one Superiority Die and take the Disengage action. You also roll the die and add the number rolled to your AC until the start of your next turn.

Feinting Attack. As a Bonus Action, you can expend one Superiority Die to feint, choosing one creature within 5 feet of yourself as your target. You have Advantage on your next attack roll against that target this turn. If that attack hits, add the Superiority Die to the attack's damage roll.

Goading Attack. When you hit a creature with an attack roll, you can expend one Superiority Die to attempt to goad the target into attacking you. Add the Superiority Die to the attack's damage roll. The target must succeed on a Wisdom saving throw or have Disadvantage on attack rolls against targets other than you until the end of your next turn.

Lunging Attack. As a Bonus Action, you can expend one Superiority Die and take the Dash action. If you move at least 5 feet in a straight line immediately before hitting with a melee attack as part of the Attack action on this turn, you can add the Superiority Die to the attack's damage roll.

Maneuvering Attack. When you hit a creature with an attack roll, you can expend one Superiority Die to maneuver one of your comrades into another position. Add the Superiority Die roll to the attack's damage roll, and choose a willing creature who can see or hear you. That creature can use its Reaction to move up to half its Speed without provoking an Opportunity Attack from the target of your attack.

Menacing Attack. When you hit a creature with an attack roll, you can expend one Superiority Die to attempt to frighten the target. Add the Superiority Die to the attack's damage roll. The target must succeed on a Wisdom saving throw or have the Frightened condition until the end of your next turn.

Precision Attack. When you miss with an attack roll, you can expend one Superiority Die, roll that die, and add it to the attack roll, potentially causing the attack to hit.

Pushing Attack. When you hit a creature with an attack roll using a weapon or an Unarmed Strike, you can expend one Superiority Die to attempt to drive the target back. Add the Superiority Die to the attack's damage roll. If the target is Large or smaller, it must succeed on a Strength saving throw or be pushed up to 15 feet directly away from you.

Riposte. When a creature misses you with a melee attack roll, you can take a Reaction and expend one Superiority Die to make a melee attack roll with a weapon or an Unarmed Strike against the creature. If you hit, add the Superiority Die to the attack's damage.

Sweeping Attack. When you hit a creature with a melee attack roll using a weapon or an Unarmed Strike, you can expend one Superiority Die to attempt to damage another creature. Choose another creature within 5 feet of the original target and within your reach. If the original attack roll would hit the second creature, it takes damage equal to the number you roll on your Superiority Die. The damage is of the same type dealt by the original attack.

Trip Attack. When you hit a creature with an attack roll using a weapon or an Unarmed Strike, you can expend one Superiority Die and add the die to the attack's damage roll. If the target is Large or smaller, it must succeed on a Strength saving throw or have the Prone condition.

features.name: Student of War
features.text: You gain proficiency in one skill of your choice from the skills available to Hunter Brutes at level 1.

features.name: Improved Combat Superiority
features.text: Your Superiority Die becomes a d10.

features.name: Relentless
features.text: Once per turn, when you use a maneuver, you can roll 1d8 and use the number rolled instead of expending a Superiority Die.

features.name: Ultimate Combat Superiority
features.text: Your Superiority Die becomes a d12.

subclasses.name: Champion
subclasses.description: Pursue Physical Excellence in Combat. A Champion focuses on the development of martial prowess in a relentless pursuit of victory. Champions combine rigorous training with physical excellence to deal devastating blows, withstand peril, and garner glory. Even in a bloody battle, Champions strive for the crown of the victor.

features.name: Improved Critical
features.text: Your attack rolls with weapons and Unarmed Strikes can score a Critical Hit on a roll of 19 or 20 on the d20.

features.name: Additional Fighting Style
features.text: You gain another Fighting Style feat of your choice.

features.name: Ferocious Warrior
features.text: The thrill of battle drives you toward victory. During combat, you can give yourself Blood Tinge whenever you start your turn without it.

features.name: Superior Critical
features.text: Your attack rolls with weapons and Unarmed Strikes can now score a Critical Hit on a roll of 18-20 on the d20.

features.name: Survivor
features.text:
You attain the pinnacle of resilience in battle, giving you these benefits.

Defy Death. You have Advantage on Death Saving Throws. Moreover, when you roll 18-20 on a Death Saving Throw, you gain the benefit of rolling a 20 on it.

Feral Rally. At the start of each of your turns, you regain Hit Points equal to 5 plus your Constitution modifier if you are Bloodied and have at least 1 Hit Point.

classes.notes: Class is built on the 5e Fighter chassis; text refers interchangeably to 'Fighter' and 'Hunter Brute' levels. Starting-equipment conflict: the Core Traits CSV lists only 'Greatsword, Shortsword and Rope', while the Players Handbook Class Overview adds 'Tool Belt, 2 Blood vial' to the front (the handbook's fuller list is used in overview.startingEquipment). OCR ligature fixes applied throughout (e.g. 'Ri e'->'Rifle', 'pro ciency'->'proficiency', 'di erent'->'different', 'nish'->'finish', 'battle eld'->'battlefield', 're ned'->'refined', 'bene t'->'benefit', 'speci ed'->'specified', 'gures'->'figures', ' ve'->'five'). The level-19 source text reads 'for which you quality' (typo for 'qualify'); corrected to match the level-4 feature wording. The CSV header misspells 'Weapon' as 'Wapon'. Champion 'Ferocious Warrior' grants 'Blood Tinge', a setting-specific resource (replaces 5e Champion's 'Heroic Warrior'/Heroic Inspiration). SOURCE CONFLICT (Sanity): the standalone class board (captured above) lists Max Sanity 12 / Sanity Die D12, but the canonical Player’s Handbook Ch.2 Core Traits list Max Sanity 12 / Sanity Die 2d6 — see master.json top-level sourceConflicts; confirm with the DM.

classes.name: Hunter Scout
classes.flavorText: Before rifles became the hunter's sacred thunder, Scouts were the first to prove that a Dreadblood could be studied, stalked, and dropped from afar. They are pathfinders, marksmen, and survivalists who read the land like scripture and kill before the dark can close its jaws.

overview.skillProficiencies: Choose 3: Animal Handling, Athletics, Stealth, Survival, Investigation or Perception

coreTraits.name: Skill Proficiencies
coreTraits.text: Choose 3: Animal Handling, Athletics, Stealth, Survival, Investigation or Perception

coreTraits.name: Starting Equipment
coreTraits.text: Hunter Rifle (unique item), Hunter Cleaver (unique item) and Pistol

features.name: Hunters Mark
features.text:
You can mark a creature with the Hunters Mark.
You can make four marks, and you regain all expended uses of this ability when you finish a Long Rest.
The number of times you can make a mark increases when you reach certain Hunter Scout levels, as shown in the Hunters Mark column of the Hunter Scout Features table.

HUNTER'S MARK
Use: Bonus Action
Range: 90 feet

You mark one creature you can see within range as your quarry. Until the mark ends, you deal an extra 1d6 damage to the target whenever you hit it with an attack roll. You also have Advantage on any Wisdom (Perception or Survival) check you make to find it.
If the target drops to 0 Hit Points before the Mark ends, you can take a Bonus Action to move the mark to a new creature you can see within range.

You can only mark one creature at a time. If you spend a Hunters Mark while another mark is active, the old mark disappears. If you take damage, you must succeed on a Constitution saving throw to maintain the Hunters Mark. The DC equals 10 or half the damage taken (round down), whichever number is higher, up to a maximum DC of 30. Your Hunters Mark ends if you have the Incapacitated condition or you die.

features.name: Weapon Mastery
features.text:
Your training with weapons allows you to use the mastery properties of two kinds of weapons of your choice with which you have proficiency.
Whenever you finish a Long Rest, you can change the kinds of weapons you chose.

features.name: Expertise
features.text: Choose one of your skill proficiencies with which you lack Expertise. You gain Expertise in that skill.

features.name: Fighting Style
features.text: You gain a Fighting Style feat of your choice (see chapter 4).

features.name: Hunter Scout Subclass
features.text: You gain a Hunter Scout subclass of your choice. The Beast Caller and the Marksman subclasses are detailed after this class's description. A subclass is a specialization that grants you features at certain Hunter Scout levels.

features.name: Ability Score Improvement
features.text: You gain the Ability Score Improvement feat (see chapter 4) or another feat of your choice for which you qualify. You gain this feature again at Hunter Scout levels 8, 12, and 16.

features.name: Extra Attack
features.text: You can attack twice instead of once whenever you take the Attack action on your turn.

features.name: Roving
features.text: Your Speed increases by 10 feet while you aren't wearing Heavy armor. You also have a Climb Speed and a Swim Speed equal to your Speed.

features.name: Expertise
features.text: Choose two of your skill proficiencies with which you lack Expertise. You gain Expertise in those skills.

features.name: Tireless
features.text:
Primal forces now help fuel you on your journeys, granting you the following benefits.
Temporary Hit Points. As an action, you can give yourself a number of Temporary Hit Points equal to 1d8 plus your Wisdom modifier (minimum of 1). You can use this action a number of times equal to your Wisdom modifier (minimum of once), and you regain all expended uses when you finish a Long Rest.
Decrease Exhaustion. Whenever you finish a Short Rest, your Exhaustion level, if any, decreases by 1.

features.name: Relentless Hunter
features.text: Taking damage can't remove your Hunter's Mark.

features.name: Precise Hunter
features.text: You have Advantage on attack rolls against the creature currently marked by your Hunter's Mark.

features.name: Feral Senses
features.text: Your instincts gives you Blindsight with a range of 30 feet.

features.name: Epic Boon
features.text: You gain an Epic Boon feat (see chapter 4) or another feat of your choice for which you qualify.

features.name: Foe Slayer
features.text: The damage die of your Hunter's Mark is a d10 rather than a d6.

subclasses.name: Beast Caller
subclasses.description: Call on a beast from beyond time. A Beast Caller forms a mystical bond with a special beast, through old knowledge signing a contract drawing on mysterious forces from the unknown.

features.name: Pact with a Beast
features.text:
You call upon a timeless beast, and through the Pact you form with it, the beast draws upon your strength for better and for worse. See the stat block: Hound of Tindalos. The beast is Friendly to you and your allies and obeys your commands. It vanishes if you die. It also vanishes if it drops to 0 Hit Points or when its stated task is done.
Summoning the Beast. You can take an action to expend a Hunters Mark to summon the beast. The Hound of Tindalos manifest in an unoccupied space that you can see within range of 90 feet. The beast will only show up to complete a specific task, either to defeat your foes during combat or something else. The reason for you to call upon it must be stated beforehand.
The Beast in Combat. In combat, the beast acts during your turn. It can move and use its Reaction on its own, but the only action it takes is the Dodge action to avoid danger, unless you take a Bonus Action to command it to take an action in its stat block or some other action. You can also sacrifice one of your attacks when you take the Attack action to command the beast to take the Beast's Strike action. If you have the Incapacitated condition, the beast acts on its own and isn't limited to the Dodge action.

HOUND OF TINDALOS
STR 14 (+2, save +2), DEX 18 (+3, save +3), CON 16 (+3, save +3), INT 4 (-3, save -3), WIS 14 (+2, save +2), CHA 5 (-3, save -3)
Medium Beast, Natural
AC 13 plus your Wisdom modifier
HP 5 plus five times your Hunter Scout level
Speed 40 ft., Climb 40 ft.
Senses Darkvision 60 ft.; Passive Perception 12
CR None (XP 0; PB equals your Proficiency Bonus)
Traits
Pact. Add your Proficiency Bonus to any ability check or saving throw the beast makes. If the beast reaches 0 Hit Points, you need to make a Grit Saving Throw DC 16. If you succeed nothing happens. If you fail loose Hit Points equals to 1d10 + your Hunter Scout level + your Wisdom modifier and suffer 4 Madness.
Actions
Beast's Feral Strike. Melee Attack Roll: Bonus equals your Wisdom modifier + your Proficiency Bonus, reach 5 ft. Hit: 2d6 + 2 + your Wisdom modifier Slashing damage. If the beast moved at least 20 feet straight toward the target before the hit, the target takes an extra 1d6 damage of Slashing damage, and the target has the Prone condition if it is a Large or smaller creature.

features.name: Deepened Pact
features.text: When you take a Bonus Action to command your summoned Hound of Tindalos to take an action, you can also command it to take the Dash, Disengage, Dodge, or Help action using its Bonus Action. In addition, whenever it hits with an attack roll and deals damage, it can deal your choice of either Psychic, Bludgeoning or Piercing damage, or its normal damage type.

features.name: The Beasts Brutality
features.text: When you command your summoned Hound of Tindalos to take the Beast's Feral Strike action, the beast can use it twice. In addition, the first time each turn it hits a creature under the effect of your Hunters Mark, the beast deals extra Piercing damage equal to the Hunters Mark's bonus damage.

features.name: Absolute Pact
features.text: Your summoned Hound of Tindalos now gains the Final Carnage action. You can now command your summoned Hound of Tindalos to make an final attack. The Final Carnage action uses the Beast's Feral Strike action four times. If you command your summoned Hound of Tindalos to take the Final Carnage action, the beast Hit Points will reach 0 at the end of the attack.

subclasses.name: Marksman
subclasses.description: You specialize in long-ranged firearms and close-quarters punishment, forcing enemies to fear both the distance between you and the mistake of closing it. Whether you unleash a storm of bullets or deliver a single shot with surgical cruelty, your rifle becomes an extension of your will. In your hands, firearms are not merely weapons, but instruments of execution, sending foes into eternal sleep before they can raise a blade.

features.name: Deadly Rifleman
features.text:
If you are using a Hunters Rifle, it now changes to Trained Hunters Rifle.
Trained Hunters Rifle — Damage: 1d12 Piercing; Properties: Ammunition (Range 150/600; Bullet), Two-Handed; Mastery: Slow; Weight: 10 lb.
If you are using a Pistol, it now changes to Trained Pistol.
Trained Pistol — Damage: 1d10 Piercing; Properties: Ammunition (Range 30/90; Bullet), Light, Loading; Mastery: Vex; Weight: 3 lb.

features.name: Hunter's Prey
features.text:
You gain one of the following feature options of your choice. Whenever you finish a Short or Long Rest, you can replace the chosen option with the other one.
Colossus Slayer. Your tenacity can wear down even the most resilient foes. When you hit a creature with a weapon, the weapon deals an extra 1d8 damage to the target if it's missing any of its Hit Points. You can deal this extra damage only once per turn.
Horde Breaker. Once on each of your turns when you make an attack with a weapon, you can make another attack with the same weapon against a different creature that is within 5 feet of the original target, that is within the weapon's range, and that you haven't attacked this turn.

features.name: Elite Rifleman
features.text: Before making an attack roll with your Trained Hunter's Rifle, you may spend 1 Hunter's Mark to gain mastery with that rifle for the attack if you do not already have it. When you do so, you may also replace the rifle's mastery property for that shot with one of the following: Topple, Sap, or Push. The chosen mastery applies only to that attack.

features.name: Superior Hunter's Prey
features.text: Once per turn when you deal damage to a creature marked by your Hunter's Mark, you can also deal that marks extra damage to a different creature that you can see within 30 feet of the first creature.

features.name: Deathmark Rifleman
features.text:
Gain the two following benefits.
Deathmark Shot. After you hit with an Attack Roll using your Trained Hunter's Rifle, you may spend 1 Hunter's Mark to turn the shot into a Deathmark Shot. Roll the attack's damage once, then apply that same damage up to three times.
You may apply all three instances of damage to the original target, or divide them among the original target and other creatures within the rifle's normal range.
Point-Blank Retort. When a creature within your pistol's range deals melee damage to you, you may use your reaction and spend 1 Hunter's Mark to immediately draw your pistol and make one pistol attack against that creature.

classes.notes: The PDF (resources/pdf/Scout.pdf) could not be rasterized in this environment (pdftoppm unavailable); content was taken from the verbatim pdftotext extract (Scout.txt) cross-checked against the Players Handbook text and the two Scout CSVs — all consistent. OCR ligature artifacts (fi/fl/ff) were repaired (e.g. 'Ri e'→'Rifle', 'pro ciency'→'proficiency', 'su er'→'suffer', 'di erent'→'different', 'bene ts'→'benefits', 'modi er'→'modifier'). The level-1 features text says features 'are listed in the Ranger Features table' — a source typo; the table is the Hunter Scout Features table (left verbatim in feature text only where it appeared, paraphrased elsewhere). The 'Ranger Features table' reference is a leftover from the 5e Ranger basis. Primary Ability (Dexterity and Wisdom) sourced from the Players Handbook Class Overview Part 1 (DEX and WIS); the Class Overview lists expanded starting equipment (Tool Belt, 1 Blood vial, 18 bullets, Hunter Rifle, Hunter Cleaver, Pistol and Bandolier) while the Core Traits table lists the shorter set (Hunter Rifle, Hunter Cleaver, Pistol) used in overview.startingEquipment. Grit appears as a saving-throw type referenced by the Hound of Tindalos Pact trait (Grit Saving Throw DC 16). Level 14 has no class feature ('—'). Source spelling 'an final attack' (Absolute Pact) and 'gives you Blindsight' (Feral Senses) preserved verbatim. SOURCE CONFLICT (Sanity): the standalone class board (captured above) lists Max Sanity 12 / Sanity Die D12, but the canonical Player’s Handbook Ch.2 Core Traits list Max Sanity 12 / Sanity Die 2d6 — see master.json top-level sourceConflicts; confirm with the DM.

classes.name: Hunter Stalker
classes.flavorText: BECOMING A HUNTER STALKER ... As a level 1 character, gain all the traits in the Core Hunter Stalker Traits table and gain the Hunter Stalker's level 1 features, which are listed in the Hunter Stalker Features table.

overview.skillProficiencies: Choose 2: Acrobatics, Athletics, Deception, Insight, Intimidation, Investigation, Perception, Sleight of Hand or Stealth
overview.weaponProficiencies: Simple weapons and Martial weapons that have the Finesse or Light property

coreTraits.name: Skill Proficiencies
coreTraits.text: Choose 2: Acrobatics, Athletics, Deception, Insight, Intimidation, Investigation, Perception, Sleight of Hand or Stealth

coreTraits.name: Weapon Proficiencies
coreTraits.text: Simple weapons and Martial weapons that have the Finesse or Light property

coreTraits.name: Starting Equipment
coreTraits.text: Scimitar, 4 Daggers, Pistol and Thieves Tools

features.name: Expertise
features.text: You gain Expertise in two of your skill proficiencies of your choice. At Hunter Stalker level 6, you gain Expertise in two more of your skill proficiencies of your choice.

features.name: Sneak Attack
features.text:
You know how to strike subtly and exploit a foe's distraction. Once per turn, you can deal an extra 1d6 damage to one creature you hit with an attack roll if you have Advantage on the roll and the attack uses a Finesse or a Ranged weapon. The extra damage's type is the same as the weapon's type.
You don't need Advantage on the attack roll if at least one of your allies is within 5 feet of the target, the ally doesn't have the Incapacitated condition, and you don't have Disadvantage on the attack roll.
The extra damage increases as you gain Hunter Stalker levels, as shown in the Sneak Attack column of the Hunter Stalker Features table.

features.name: Weapon Mastery
features.text:
Your training with weapons allows you to use the mastery properties of two kinds of weapons of your choice with which you have proficiency.
Whenever you finish a Long Rest, you can change the kinds of weapons you chose.

features.name: Cunning Action
features.text: Your quick thinking and agility allow you to move and act quickly. On your turn, you can take one of the following actions as a Bonus Action: Dash, Disengage, or Hide.

features.name: Hunter Stalker Subclass
features.text: You gain a Hunter Stalker subclass of your choice. The Assassin or the Thief subclasses are detailed after this class's description. A subclass is a specialization that grants you features at certain Hunter Stalker levels.

features.name: Steady Aim
features.text: As a Bonus Action, you give yourself Advantage on your next attack roll on the current turn. You can use this feature only if you haven't moved during this turn, and after you use it, your Speed is 0 until the end of the current turn.

features.name: Ability Score Improvement
features.text: You gain the Ability Score Improvement feat (see chapter 4) or another feat of your choice for which you qualify. You gain this feature again at Hunter Stalker levels 8, 10, 12, and 16.

features.name: Cunning Strike
features.text:
You've developed cunning ways to use your Sneak Attack. When you deal Sneak Attack damage, you can add one of the following Cunning Strike effects.
Each effect has a die cost, which is the number of Sneak Attack damage dice you must forgo to add the effect. You remove the die before rolling, and the effect occurs immediately after the attack's damage is dealt. For example, if you add the Poison effect, remove 1d6 from the Sneak Attack's damage before rolling.
If a Cunning Strike effect requires a saving throw, the DC equals 8 plus your Dexterity modifier and Proficiency Bonus.
Poison (Cost: 1d6). You add a toxin to your strike, forcing the target to make a Constitution saving throw. On a failed save, the target has the Poisoned condition for 1 minute. At the end of each of its turns, the Poisoned target repeats the save, ending the effect on itself on a success. To use this effect, you must have a Poisoner's Kit on your person.
Trip (Cost: 1d6). If the target is Large or smaller, it must succeed on a Dexterity saving throw or have the Prone condition.
Withdraw (Cost: 1d6). Immediately after the attack, you move up to half your Speed without provoking Opportunity Attacks.

features.name: Evasion
features.text: You can nimbly dodge out of the way of certain dangers. When you're subjected to an effect that allows you to make a Dexterity saving throw to take only half damage, you instead take no damage if you succeed on the saving throw and only half damage if you fail. You can't use this feature if you have the Incapacitated condition.

features.name: Reliable Talent
features.text: Whenever you make an ability check that uses one of your skill or tool proficiencies, you can treat a d20 roll of 9 or lower as a 10.

features.name: Improved Cunning Strike
features.text: You can use up to two Cunning Strike effects when you deal Sneak Attack damage, paying the die cost for each effect.

features.name: Devious Strikes
features.text:
You've practiced new ways to use your Sneak Attack deviously. The following effects are now among your Cunning Strike options.
Daze (Cost: 2d6). The target must succeed on a Constitution saving throw, or on its next turn, it can do only one of the following: move or take an action or a Bonus Action.
Knock Out (Cost: 6d6). The target must succeed on a Constitution saving throw, or it has the Unconscious condition for 1 minute or until it takes any damage. The Unconscious target repeats the save at the end of each of its turns, ending the effect on itself on a success.
Obscure (Cost: 3d6). The target must succeed on a Dexterity saving throw, or it has the Blinded condition until the end of its next turn.

features.name: Slippery Mind
features.text: Your cunning mind is exceptionally difficult to control. You gain proficiency in Wisdom and Charisma saving throws.

features.name: Elusive
features.text: You're so evasive that attackers rarely gain the upper hand against you. No attack roll can have Advantage against you unless you have the Incapacitated condition.

features.name: Epic Boon
features.text: You gain an Epic Boon feat (see chapter 4) or another feat of your choice for which you qualify.

features.name: Stroke of Luck
features.text:
You have a marvelous knack for succeeding when you need to. If you fail a D20 Test, you can turn the roll into a 20.
Once you use this feature, you can't use it again until you finish a Short or Long Rest.

subclasses.name: Assassin
subclasses.description: Practice the Grim Art of Death. An Assassin's training focuses on using stealth, poison, and disguise to eliminate foes with deadly efficiency. While some Hunter Stalkers who follow this path are hired killers, spies, or bounty hunters, the capabilities of this subclass are equally useful for adventurers facing a variety of monstrous enemies.

features.name: Assassinate
features.text:
You're adept at ambushing a target, granting you the following benefits.
Initiative. You have Advantage on Initiative rolls.
Surprising Strikes. During the first round of each combat, you have Advantage on attack rolls against any creature that hasn't taken a turn. If your Sneak Attack hits any target during that round, the target takes extra damage of the weapon's type equal to your Hunter Stalker level.

features.name: Infiltration Expertise
features.text:
You are expert at the following techniques that aid your infiltrations.
Masterful Mimicry. You can unerringly mimic another person's speech, handwriting, or both. To mimic a person's speech, you must have heard them speak and spent 1 action studying their voice. To mimic a person's handwriting, you must have seen their writing and spent 1 action studying it.
Roving Aim. Your Speed isn't reduced to 0 by using Steady Aim.

features.name: Envenom Weapons
features.text: When you use the Poison option of your Cunning Strike, the target also takes 2d6 Poison damage whenever it fails the saving throw. This damage ignores Resistance to Poison damage.

features.name: Death Strike
features.text: When you hit with your Sneak Attack on the first round of a combat, the target must succeed on a Constitution saving throw (DC 8 plus your Dexterity modifier and Proficiency Bonus), or the attack's damage is doubled against the target.

subclasses.name: Shadow
subclasses.description: Hunt for Treasure as a Classic Adventurer. A mix of burglar, treasure hunter, and explorer, you are the epitome of an adventurer. In addition to improving your agility and stealth, you gain abilities useful for delving into ruins and getting maximum benefit from the magic items you find there.

features.name: Fast Hands
features.text:
As a Bonus Action, you can do one of the following.
Sleight of Hand. Make a Dexterity (Sleight of Hand) check to pick a lock or disarm a trap with Thieves' Tools or to pick a pocket.
Use an Object. Take the Utilize action.

features.name: Second-Story Work
features.text:
You've trained to get into especially hard-to-reach places, granting you these benefits.
Climber. You gain a Climb Speed equal to your Speed.
Jumper. You can determine your jump distance using your Dexterity rather than your Strength.

features.name: Supreme Sneak
features.text:
You gain the following Cunning Strike option.
Stealth Attack (Cost: 1d6). If you have the Hide action's Invisible condition, this attack doesn't end that condition on you if you end the turn behind Three-Quarters Cover or Total Cover.

features.name: Cloak of Shadows
features.text: As a Bonus Action, you can give yourself the Invisible condition until the end of your next turn. You can use this feature a number of times equal to your Wisdom modifier (minimum of once), and you regain all expended uses when you finish a Long Rest.

features.name: Shadowbringer
features.text: You are adept at laying ambushes and quickly escaping danger. You can take two turns during the first round of any combat. You take your first turn at your normal Initiative and your second turn at your Initiative minus 10.

classes.notes: HIT DIE CONFLICT: The Core Hunter Stalker Traits table (both Classes-Core Hunter Stalker Traits.csv and Classes Boards for send.txt) lists the Hit Point Die as D10 per Hunter Stalker level. The Players Handbook Class Overview (Part 1) lists the Stalker Hit Die as D8. The project README resolves this in favor of D8 (per the 5e Rogue basis), so overview.hitDie is set to D8; the class-specific Core Traits value (D10) is preserved verbatim in coreTraits. Confirm with the DM. SUBCLASS NAMING: The class body twice refers to the second subclass / available subclasses as 'Assassin or the Thief subclasses', but the actual second subclass section is titled SHADOW (Thief-equivalent); recorded as 'Shadow'. The level-3 subclass feature text literally says 'The Assassin or the Thief subclasses are detailed after this class's description' (verbatim, despite the section being named Shadow). EQUIPMENT: The Players Handbook Class Overview lists fuller starting gear ('Tool Belt, 1 Blood vial, 4 bullets, Scimitar, 4 Daggers, Pistol and Thieves Tools') while the Core Traits table lists only 'Scimitar, 4 Daggers, Pistol and Thieves Tools'; overview.startingEquipment uses the Core Traits list. OCR FIXES applied: 'proficiency'/'Proficiency', 'finish', 'effect(s)', 'efficiency', 'difficult', 'modifier', 'benefit(s)', 'infiltration', 'first', 'find', 'Rifle' (in handbook overview row). The PDF rendered Steady Aim's Speed as 'O' (letter O); corrected to '0' (zero) per rules intent. The CSV Features table has typos preserved in source ('Repliable Tallent' = Reliable Talent, 'Wapon' = Weapon) — normalized in this fragment. Level 19 source text reads 'for which you quality' (OCR for 'qualify'); corrected. 'at-tack'/'con-trol' hyphenation artifacts removed. SOURCE CONFLICT (Sanity): the standalone class board (captured above) lists Max Sanity 10 / Sanity Die D10, but the canonical Player’s Handbook Ch.2 Core Traits list Max Sanity 12 / Sanity Die 1d12 — see master.json top-level sourceConflicts; confirm with the DM.

classes.name: Hunter Deepcaller
classes.flavorText: Most Deepcallers seek power through forbidden study, reading from the Book of the Deepcaller and calling upon many Patrons. Through occult rituals, the Deepcaller has expanded their mind beyond mortal limits, gaining the ability to comprehend eldritch passages and speak words no sane creature was meant to understand.

overview.skillProficiencies: Choose 2: Eldrich Knowledge, Old World History, Investigation, Insight, Blood Nature, Religion or Deception

coreTraits.name: Skill Proficiencies
coreTraits.text: Choose 2: Eldrich Knowledge, Old World History, Investigation, Insight, Blood Nature, Religion or Deception

coreTraits.name: Starting Equipment
coreTraits.text: Sickle, 2 Daggers, Book (eldrich knowledge) and Robe

features.name: Eldritch Comprehension
features.text:
Through occult rituals, you have expanded your mind beyond mortal limits, gaining the ability to comprehend eldritch passages and speak words no sane creature was meant to understand. These forbidden words allow you to read and perform Rites.

See at the end of this class description for the rules on Rite-performing. The information below details how you use those rules with Hunter Deepcaller Rites, which appear in Appendix B “The Book of the Deepcaller” And Appendix C “Hushed Whispers”.

features.name: Fracturing Mind
features.text: Every time you level up suffer 2 Madness and permanently gain 1 max Sanity.

features.name: Braced Mind
features.text:
As an Action, you steady your mind through an esoteric rite, forcing forbidden knowledge back into shape.
When you do so, you suffer 1 Madness and regain a number of expended Strains equal to half your maximum number of Strains, rounded up.
Once you use this feature, you cannot use it again until you finish a Long Rest.

features.name: Vailed Truth
features.text: You no longer suffer Madness from performing Level 1 Rites unless an effect specifically says so.

features.name: The Hunter Zealot Prestige Class
features.text:
At 3rd level, you may enter the Path of the Zealot Prestige Class, abandoning the traditional path of the Deepcaller.
If you choose this Prestige Class, you gain no further features from the Hunter Deepcaller class. Instead, whenever you would gain a Hunter Deepcaller feature, you gain the corresponding feature from the Path of the Zealot Prestige Class.
You lose all Hunter Deepcaller features gained before entering this Prestige Class unless a Zealot feature specifically says otherwise.
The Path of the Zealot Prestige Class is detailed after the Hunter Deepcaller class description.
A Prestige Class is an alternative progression path that replaces your existing class path with a specialized set of abilities.

features.name: Opened Mind
features.text:
As a Bonus Action, you can establish a telepathic link between yourself and up to eight willing creatures you can see within 60 feet.
The link lasts for 1 round (the start of your next round). While linked, each creature may communicate telepathically with the others. When the link is established, each linked creature may contribute once to the conversation. The order is determined by the participants themselves. Once a creature has finished communicating, the next creature may speak.
This communication occurs outside the normal flow of time and does not consume Actions, Bonus Actions, Reactions, or movement.
Once you use this feature, you can't use it again until you finish a Short or Long Rest unless you expend a Strain (no action required) to restore your use of it.

features.name: Ability Score Improvement
features.text: You gain the Ability Score Improvement feat (see chapter 4) or another feat of your choice for which you qualify. You gain this feature again at Hunter Deepcaller levels 8, 12, and 16.

features.name: Endured Truth
features.text: You no longer suffer Madness from performing Level 2 Rites unless an effect specifically says so.

features.name: Opened Mind Combatant
features.text:
When you use your Opened Mind feature, you may choose one creature within 60 feet that you can see. The creature must succeed on a Wisdom saving throw against your Rite Save DC.
On a failed save, you glimpse fragments of its immediate future and perceive its intentions before they unfold.
For the duration of 10 rounds, the chosen creature has Disadvantage on attack rolls against you, and you have Advantage on attack rolls against it.

features.name: Accepted Truth
features.text: You no longer suffer Madness from performing Level 3 Rites unless an effect specifically says so.

features.name: Fragments of a Eldritch Mind
features.text: You have Resistance to Mind damage, and whenever a creature deals Mind damage to you, that creature takes the same amount of damage that you take.

features.name: Forbidden Revelation
features.text:
Your studies uncover a fragment of knowledge that few minds can endure. The Book of the Deepcaller begins to reveal hidden passages that were always there, but impossible for you to perceive until now.
Choose one Level 6 Rite. This Rite becomes one of your Forbidden Revelations.
You can perform this Rite once without expending Strain. Once you perform it in this way, you cannot do so again until you finish a Long Rest.
At Hunter Deepcaller levels 13, 15, and 17, the Book reveals another Forbidden Revelation to you. These Revelations must be chosen from Hunter Deepcaller Rites of the following levels:
Level 13: one Level 7 Rite
Level 15: one Level 8 Rite
Level 17: one Level 9 Rite
You can also use a Forbidden Revelation for a 1-5 Level Rite that has the option to use Higher Level Strains. That Rite then becomes the level of which Forbidden Revelation you choose.
Whenever you finish a Long Rest, you may replace all of your Forbidden Revelations with another Hunter Deepcaller Rite of the same level.
You regain all uses of your Forbidden Revelations when you finish a Long Rest.

features.name: Embraced Truth
features.text: You no longer suffer Madness from performing Level 4 Rites unless an effect specifically says so.

features.name: Great Summoning
features.text:
When you perform Call Lesser Starborn, you can choose to forgo Concentration. If you do, the Rite's duration becomes 10 rounds, and the summoned Lesser Starborn gains Temporary Hit Points equal to your Hunter Deepcaller level + your Intelligence modifier.
In addition, the first time each turn the Lesser Starborn hits a creature affected by your Eldritch Chain of Bolts, the attack deals an extra 1d6 Mind damage.
The first time the Lesser Starborn hits a creature affected by your Eldritch Chain of Bolts, choose one Ability. For as long as that creature remains affected by your Eldritch Chain of Bolts, it has Disadvantage on Ability Checks made using that chosen Ability.

features.name: Absolute Truth
features.text: You no longer suffer Madness from performing Level 5 Rites unless an effect specifically says so.

features.name: Epic Boon
features.text: You gain an Epic Boon feat (see chapter 4) or another feat of your choice for which you qualify.

features.name: Eldritch Mastery
features.text: When you use your Braced Mind feature, you regain all of your expended Strains instead of only half.

ritePerformingRules.name: Whispers
ritePerformingRules.text:
You know two Whispers of your choice. Whispers are lesser fragments of forbidden knowledge that are not written in the Book of the Deepcaller and must be memorized.
Whenever you gain a Hunter Deepcaller level, you can replace one Whisper you know with another Whisper of your choice.
When you reach Hunter Deepcaller levels 4 and 10, you learn another Whisper of your choice, as shown in the Whispers column of the Hunter Deepcaller Features table.

ritePerformingRules.name: Strains
ritePerformingRules.text:
The Hunter Deepcaller Features table shows how many Strains you have to perform your Rites of Levels 1–5. The table also shows the level of those Strains, all of which are the same level. You regain all expended Strains when you finish a Short or Long Rest.
For example, when you are a level 5 Hunter Deepcaller, you have two Level 3 Strains. To perform a Level 1 Rite, you must spend one of those Strains, and the Rite is performed as a Level 3 Rite.

ritePerformingRules.name: The Book of the Deepcaller
ritePerformingRules.text:
Your Book of the Deepcaller contains the forbidden passages needed to perform Hunter Deepcaller Rites of level 1-5. You do not prepare these Rites. Any Hunter Deepcaller Rite in your book is available for you to perform, provided you have a Strain of a high enough level to perform it.
As your Strain level increases, your mind becomes capable of comprehending deeper and more dangerous passages within the book. You can perform any Hunter Deepcaller Rite of a level equal to or lower than your current Strain level.
For example, when your Strain level is 3, you can perform Hunter Deepcaller Rites of Levels 1, 2, and 3, but not Rites of Levels 4 or 5.

ritePerformingRules.name: Rite Performing Ability
ritePerformingRules.text: Intelligence is the Rite Performing ability for your Hunter Deepcaller Rites.

ritePerformingRules.name: Performing a Rite
ritePerformingRules.text: Unless otherwise stated, performing a Rite requires you to hold a Book of the Deepcaller in one hand and speak the Rite's words aloud. Each time you perform a Rite, you suffer 2 Madness, unless a feature or effect says otherwise.

ritePerformingRules.name: Performing a Whisper
ritePerformingRules.text: Whispers are an exception to the normal rules for performing Rites. Performing a Whisper requires only a hushed recitation of its words. You do not need a Book of the Deepcaller to perform a Whisper, and performing a Whisper does not cause you to suffer Madness unless an effect says otherwise.

ritePerformingRules.name: Break the Limits of Your Mind
ritePerformingRules.text: If you perform a Rite using a Strain level higher than your current Strain level, you suffer Madness equal to your maximum Sanity immediately after the Rite is performed. This is not recommended.

ritePerformingRules.name: Types of Rites
ritePerformingRules.text: Each Rite has a type. The types are listed in the Types of Rites table. These categories help describe Rites but have no rules of their own, although some other rules refer to them.

ritePerformingRules.name: Longer Performing Than One Action
ritePerformingRules.text:
Certain Rites require more Actions to perform. While you perform a Rite with a Performing of two or more Actions, you must take the Rite action on each of your turns until the requirement is met, and you must maintain Concentration (see further down) while you do so.
If your Concentration is broken, the Rite fails, but you don't expend a Strain. To perform the Rite again, you must start over.

ritePerformingRules.name: Range
ritePerformingRules.text:
A Rite’s range indicates how far from the performer of the Rite the Rites effect can originate, and the Rites description specifies which part of the effect is limited by the range.
A range usually takes one of the following forms:
Distance. The range is expressed in feet.
Touch. The Rite’s effect originates on something, as defined by the Rite, that the performer of the Rite must touch within their reach.
Self. The Rite is performed on the performer self, or emanates from them, as specified in the Rite.
If a Rite has movable effects, they aren't restricted by its range unless the Rite’s description says otherwise.

ritePerformingRules.name: Duration
ritePerformingRules.text:
A Rites's duration is the length the Rites persists after it is performed. A duration typically takes one of the following forms:
Concentration. A duration that requires Concentration follows the Concentration rules. (see further down).
Instantaneous. An instantaneous duration means the Rites's outcome appears only for a moment and then disappears.
Span. A duration that provides a span specifies how many rounds the Rite lasts. While span Rite that you perform is ongoing, you can dismiss it (no action required) if you don't have the Incapacitated condition or it is a Summoning Rite.

ritePerformingRules.name: Concentration
ritePerformingRules.text:
Some Rites and other effects require Concentration to remain active, as specified in their descriptions.
If the effect's creator loses Concentration, the effect ends. If the effect has a maximum duration, the effect's description specifies for how many rounds the performer can concentrate on it. The creator can end Concentration at any time (no action required).
The following factors break Concentration.
Another Concentration Effect. You lose Concentration on an effect the moment you start performing a Rite that requires Concentration or activate another effect that requires Concentration.
Damage. If you take damage, you must succeed on a Constitution saving throw to maintain Concentration. The DC equals 10 or half the damage taken (round down), whichever number is higher, up to a maximum DC of 30.
Incapacitated or Dead. Your Concentration ends if you have the Incapacitated condition or you die.

ritePerformingRules.name: Rite Effects
ritePerformingRules.text: The effects of a Rite are detailed after its Duration entry. These effects describe exactly what the Rite does, often ignoring the normal laws of nature. Any outcomes beyond those effects are determined by the DM. A Rite's effects typically involve targets, saving throws, Rite attacks, or a combination of the three.

ritePerformingRules.name: Targets
ritePerformingRules.text:
Most Rites require you to choose one or more targets. A Rite's description specifies whether it targets creatures, objects, areas, or something else.
A Clear Path to the Target. To target something with a Rite, you must have a clear path to it. A target behind Total Cover cannot be targeted unless the Rite states otherwise.
Targeting Yourself. If a Rite targets a creature of your choice, you may choose yourself unless the Rite specifies a hostile creature or a creature other than yourself.
Areas of Effect. Some Rites affect an area rather than a specific target. The Rite's description specifies the shape of the affected area, which is typically one of the following: Cone, Cube, Cylinder, Emanation, Line, Sphere. A creature or object within the area is affected as described by the Rite.
Awareness of Being Targeted. Unless a Rite produces an obvious effect, a creature is unaware it was targeted. Effects such as lightning, explosions, or summoned horrors are obvious. More subtle effects, such as reading thoughts or influencing the mind, typically go unnoticed unless the Rite states otherwise.
Invalid Targets. If you perform a Rite on a creature or object that cannot be affected by it, the Rite has no effect on that target. Any Strain spent to perform the Rite is still expended.
If the Rite normally allows a saving throw and has no effect on a successful save, an invalid target appears to have succeeded on its saving throw. Otherwise, you perceive that the Rite had no effect.

ritePerformingRules.name: Saving Throws
ritePerformingRules.text:
Many Rites allow a target to make a saving throw to avoid some or all of their effects. The Rite specifies which ability is used and what happens on a success or failure. The saving throw DC for your Rites is calculated as follows:
Rite Save DC = 8 + your Rite Performing Ability Modifier + your Proficiency Bonus
Your Rite Performing Ability is determined by your class.

ritePerformingRules.name: Rite Attacks
ritePerformingRules.text:
Some Rites require you to make a Rite Attack to determine whether the Rite hits its target. Your Rite Attack modifier is calculated as follows:
Rite Attack Modifier = your Rite Performing Ability Modifier + your Proficiency Bonus

ritePerformingRules.name: Combining Rite Effects
ritePerformingRules.text: The effects of different Rites stack while their durations overlap. However, the effects of the same Rite do not stack with themselves. If the same Rite affects a target multiple times, only the most potent effect applies. If multiple instances are equally potent, the most recent effect applies. For example, if two Deepcallers perform the same Rite on the same target, the target benefits from only one instance of that Rite unless the Rite specifically states otherwise.

prestigeClass.name: Path of the Zealot
prestigeClass.description: Most Deepcallers seek power through forbidden study, reading from the Book of the Deepcaller and calling upon many Patrons. A Zealot abandons that path. Through blood, fire, and devotion, the Zealot offers the book to a single eldritch being and becomes its living weapon. A Zealot does not search for many truths. A Zealot serves one Truth.

features.name: Burn the Book
features.text:
When you enter the Hunter Zealot Prestige Class, your Book of the Deepcaller is destroyed, burned, consumed, and claimed by your new Patron.
You lose all Hunter Deepcaller features gained before entering this Prestige Class unless a Zealot feature specifically says otherwise. You gain no further features from the Hunter Deepcaller class. Instead, whenever you would gain a Hunter Deepcaller feature, you gain the corresponding feature from the Hunter Zealot Prestige Class.
You can no longer perform Rites of Level 2 or higher. You also lose all Rites of Level 2 or higher that you knew or had prepared.
You retain your Rite Performing ability, Rite Save DC, and Rite Attack modifier.

features.name: Chosen of One Patron
features.text:
Your new Patron becomes the source of your Zealot features.
You no longer require a Book of the Deepcaller to perform Whispers. The words of your Patron are carved into your mind, blood, and flesh.

features.name: Zealot Whispers
features.text:
You retain the Whispers you knew before entering this Prestige Class.
In addition, when you prepare Whispers, you can choose from both normal Whispers and Level 1 Hunter Deepcaller Rites. A Level 1 Rite prepared in this way becomes a Zealot Whisper for you.
A Zealot Whisper:
does not require a Book of the Deepcaller,
does not expend Strain,
does not cause you to suffer Madness,
cannot be performed using Higher-Level Strain,
uses your Rite Save DC and Rite Attack modifier as normal.
You prepare a number of Whispers equal to the number shown for a Hunter Deepcaller of your level, plus one additional Whisper.

features.name: Zealot Training
features.text:
Your Patron reshapes you for battle.
You gain proficiency with martial weapons.

features.name: Eldritch Armament
features.text:
Your devotion allows your Patron to guide your weapon through your body.
When you attack with a weapon with which you have proficiency, you can use your Rite Performing ability for the attack and damage rolls instead of Strength or Dexterity.
When you hit with such a weapon, you can choose for the weapon to deal its normal damage type or Eldritch Power damage.

features.name: Carved Eldritch Strike
features.text: You always have Eldritch Strike prepared. It does not count against the number of Whispers you can prepare.

features.name: Carved Armor of Agathys
features.text: You always have Armor of Agathys prepared. For you, Armor of Agathys is treated as a Whisper. It does not count against the number of Whispers you can prepare.

features.name: Strain of Devotion
features.text:
You no longer use Strain to perform Rites. Instead, your Strain becomes a weaponized expression of your devotion.
Your Strain progression and maximum Strain level continue to improve as shown in the Hunter Deepcaller table. However, you can spend Strain only on Zealot features.
You gain the following ways to spend Strain:
Patron's Blow. Once per turn, when you hit a creature with a weapon attack or Eldritch Strike, you can expend one Strain to deal extra Eldritch Power damage to the target. The extra damage equals 1d8 per level of the Strain expended.
Patron's Ward. When you take damage, you can take a Reaction and expend one Strain to reduce the damage by 1d8 per level of the Strain expended + your Rite Performing ability modifier.
Patron's Step. As a Bonus Action, you can expend one Strain to teleport up to 10 feet per level of the Strain expended to an unoccupied space you can see. This movement does not provoke opportunity attacks.

features.name: Ability Score Improvement
features.text: You gain the Ability Score Improvement feat (see chapter 4) or another feat of your choice for which you qualify. You gain this feature again at Hunter Zealot levels 8, 12, and 16.

features.name: Rite and Blade
features.text:
You can attack twice, instead of once, whenever you take the Attack Action on your turn.
In addition, when you use your Action to perform a Whisper, you can make one weapon attack as a Bonus Action.

features.name: Fanatical Focus
features.text:
Your devotion shields your mind from hesitation, fear, and pain.
When you fail a saving throw, you can expend one Strain to reroll the saving throw. You must use the new roll.
If the new roll succeeds, you can immediately move up to half your Speed without provoking opportunity attacks.

features.name: Zealous Presence
features.text:
As a Bonus Action, you unleash a command filled with the authority of your Patron.
Choose up to eight creatures of your choice within 60 feet that can see or hear you. Until the start of your next turn, each chosen creature has Advantage on attack rolls and saving throws.
Once you use this feature, you cannot use it again until you finish a Long Rest, unless you expend one Strain to use it again.

features.name: Epic Boon
features.text: You gain an Epic Boon feat (see chapter 4) or another feat of your choice for which you qualify.

features.name: Absolute Union
features.text:
As a Bonus Action, you allow your Patron to fully manifest through your body. This transformation lasts for 5 rounds, until you drop to 0 Hit Points, or until you end it early as a Bonus Action.
Once you use this feature, you cannot use it again until you finish a Long Rest.
When you enter this form, you suffer 20 Madness.
While transformed, you gain the following benefits:
Patron's Form. Your body becomes a living vessel for your Patron. Your eyes turn black like the abyss, blackened veins spread across your flesh, impossible shadows cling to your body, bone spines tear through your skin, and spectral limbs writhe around you as if guided by a will beyond your own. When you transform, each other creature within a 30-foot-radius Sphere centered on you must make a Wisdom saving throw against your Rite Save DC.
On a failed save, a creature takes 8d10 Mind damage and suffers 6 Madness. On a successful save, a creature takes half as much damage and suffers 2 Madness.
Each other creature within 120 feet that can see your transformation unfold, but is outside the 30-foot-radius Sphere, suffers 2 Madness.
Eldritch Resistance. You have Immunity to Mind damage and Resistance to Eldritch Power damage.
Unshaken Vessel. You cannot be Frightened, Charmed, Paralyzed, or Stunned.
Devoted Assault. The first time you hit a creature on each of your turns with a weapon attack or Eldritch Strike, the target takes an extra 4d8 Eldritch Power damage.
Lashing Tendrils. As an Action, you command the spectral limbs of your Patron to lash out. Choose up to three creatures you can see within 30 feet of you. Each target must make a Strength saving throw against your Rite Save DC.
On a failed save, a target takes 6d10 Eldritch Power damage and gains the Restrained condition. On a successful save, a target takes half as much damage and is not Restrained.
A Restrained creature can repeat the saving throw at the end of each of its turns, ending the condition on itself on a success.
You cannot use Lashing Tendrils again while a creature is Restrained by this feature.
Tendril Reprisal. When a creature within 15 feet of you hits you with an attack, you can take a Reaction to lash out with a spectral tendril. The creature takes 3d10 Eldritch Power damage and must succeed on a Strength saving throw against your Rite Save DC or have the Restrained condition until the end of its next turn.
Unbroken Vessel. If you would drop to 0 Hit Points while this transformation is active, you can expend one Strain to drop to 1 Hit Point instead.
If you drop to 0 Hit Points and do not use this benefit, the transformation ends.

classes.notes: The Hunter Deepcaller has no standard subclasses; at level 3 the only branching option is the optional Path of the Zealot Prestige Class (captured in prestigeClass), which replaces all Deepcaller features. Source spellings preserved: 'Vailed Truth', 'Eldrich Knowledge', 'Eldrich knowledge', 'Wapon' (Weapon), 'Fragments of a Eldritch Mind'. OCR ligature artifacts (su er/e ect/speci ed/nish/modi er/pro ciency/Ri e/ ow/ esh/ re/in uencing/rst/lled) were repaired. The Deepcaller PDF (resources/pdf/Deepcaller.pdf) could not be rendered as images in this environment (pdftoppm unavailable), so content was taken from the extracted text plus CSV tables, which are mutually consistent. Players Handbook Class Overview confirms: primary ability INT, saving throws INT+WIS, Hit Die D6, speed 30ft (6 sq); the Handbook overview lists starting equipment with an added 'Tool Belt, 1 Blood vial' prefix ('Tool Belt, 1 Blood vial, Sickle, 2 Daggers, Book (eldrich knowledge) and Robe') versus the class traits table ('Sickle, 2 Daggers, Book (eldrich knowledge) and Robe'); the class traits table value is used here. Level 6 features table lists 'Opened Mind Combatant,' with a trailing comma (no second feature). The general Rite-performing rules section ('THE DEEPCALLERS RITES') is included in ritePerformingRules for completeness; actual Rite/Whisper spell lists (Appendix B/C) are out of scope per instructions.
